result:
The water in the kettle trembled like a secret about to be told. I watched it from the kitchen stool, elbows on the blue-checked oilcloth, chin in hands. The rest of the flat was dark; only the range light glowed, turning the linoleum into a small yellow lake. Every surface held the hush that arrives after midnight, when even the mice hesitate, unsure whether the night is still theirs to claim.

I was waiting for the boil because it gave me a task, something to tether the next thirty seconds to the ones that came before. Without that silver bubble rising, I would have to admit I was sitting here at two-forty-one on a Wednesday because I’d forgotten how to sleep. Not forgotten in the romantic, white-shirted, tragic-poet way—no, this was a duller failure. The bed was warm; the pillow had the correct density; the blinds kept the streetlamp at bay. Yet when I closed my eyes, the reels began: conversations I had mangled, invoices I had forgotten to attach, the slow-motion replay of a stray pigeon flying into the patio door and my own startled reflection behind the glass, arms flapping like torn flags. The reels never ended, they only paused, politely, when I opened my eyes again. Then they queued up, humming, like commuters who know the next train will be theirs.

Steam arrived first, ghost-white ribbons. The kettle clicked, then roared. I poured the water over the last bag of chamomile, watching it bob and drown. The cup was the sturdy hotel kind I’d “borrowed” years earlier, its rim chipped into a miniature white cliff. Somewhere in the city an ambulance braided its way through traffic, the siren thinning and widening—human grief riding radio waves. I counted the seconds until the sound vanished. Eight. Nine. Ten. Gone.

The tea smelled like wet hay and boredom. I carried it to the window, pushed the sash up an inch, and let January crawl inside. The cold pinched my wrists, a bright reminder that bodies still register borders: inside, outside, alive, not. Across the alley, a single window glowed. A man stood there in a T-shirt, lifting hand weights, counting reps I couldn’t hear. His face was clenched with the effort, but his eyes stared straight ahead, fixed on some invisible horizon where biceps never tire and the clock quits. We were two candles in our separate holders, burning at either end of the same darkness. I raised my cup to him; he did not see.

My phone blinked on the counter—another headline, another outrage magnet. I left it face-down; I already knew the world was ending in the same way I knew the sun would rise: an abstract certainty that had no bearing on whether the bin (overflowing) would fit one more yogurt cup. Doom is scalable; dishes are not.

The tea cooled enough to sip. It tasted like apology letters never sent. I thought of my mother, how she used to say, “Nothing good happens after two A.M.” She meant trouble—car crashes, bad decisions, blood on asphalt—but I’ve learned the hour holds quieter disasters: the moment you realize you have become the kind of person who talks to kettles, who catalogs the exact number of ceiling cracks (twelve), who rehearses tomorrow’s smile in the microwave door’s black glass as if facial muscles were a foreign language you once spoke fluently and now must memorize again.

I padded to the hallway, floorboards creaking Morse code under the rug. The junk drawer gaped open, a mouth with too many teeth: rubber bands, expired passports, a single AA battery rolling like a drunk. Buried beneath take-out menus was the envelope. Manila, 4x9, bruised at the corners. My name still legible in the dead handwriting of an ex who believed lowercase letters were humbler, more honest. I hadn’t opened it in three house-moves, four phones, two diets, one pandemic. Tonight my fingers obeyed before the reels could object. Inside: a strip of photo-booth pictures, the colors bleeding toward violet. We’re crammed into the frame, cheeks fused, laughing so hard the camera captured a blur of teeth and tonsils. On the back, in the same lowercase: “proof that we were ever that.”

I sat on the floorboards, legs splayed like a discarded puppet. The photos radiated heat, impossible after all these years. I touched the glossy surface; my fingertip came away dry. Yet something moved through me, a sudden draught lifting the yellow curtains of my ribs. I remembered the day: County Fair, ferris wheel stuck mid-spin, rain coming in sideways. We’d sprinted to the photo booth, soaked, shoes squelching, feeding wrinkled dollars into the slot. Between flashes, he’d kissed the rain from my eyelashes and said, “Stay right here forever.” We didn’t, of course. Forever turned out to be five more months, one fierce argument about groceries, a slammed door that echoed so long it became the soundtrack to everything that wasn’t us.

I thought I’d kept the envelope to remind me how happiness curdles. But tonight the pictures looked different: two strangers mid-ha-ha, immortalized not because love lasts but because light does. The camera caught photons, not promises. The laughter wasn’t a lie; it was simply Tuesday. And Tuesdays end. That’s what they do.

A sharp crack startled me: the building settling, or maybe the sound of my own heart adjusting to a new altitude. I returned to the window. Weight-lifter had vanished, his light snapped off. The alley was a tunnel of black ink. Overhead, clouds scudded, breaking apart like nations before war. Somewhere inside them, the moon kept its own time. I closed the sash, pressed my forehead to the cool glass, and whispered the only spell I still believed: “Tomorrow is not yet; today is almost; now is enough.”

I drank the last of the tea, lukewarm, tasting nothing but water that had once been near a flower. Then I tore the photos into tiny pieces, each shard smaller than a fingernail, and let them snow into the bin on top of the yogurt cup. The gesture felt neither cathartic nor tragic—just accurate, like marking the height of a child on a doorframe and acknowledging the wall will outlast both of you.

Back in bed, I lay on my side, knees drawn, the position of someone hiding in a cupboard during a storm. The reels tried to queue, but I pictured the pigeon again—this time in slow, forgiving motion: the soft thud, the stunned blink, the eventual flutter skyward. We were both startled, both alive, both given another chance to choose a different window. I closed my eyes. The kettle cooled in the kitchen, clicking its metallic syllables as steel contracts. Outside, the city kept exhaling, a great lung that never quite empties. Somewhere, a siren started. I counted: one, two—by seven, I was already gone.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
